tone_and_voice: Grounded, practical, value-chain-literate. Agriculture writing should speak in terms of production, input access, and market linkage -- avoid abstract 'rural development' language in favor of specific agronomic and economic terms farmers themselves would recognize.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must distinguish production-stage findings (yield, inputs) from market-stage findings (pricing, aggregation) explicitly -- a common analytical error in agriculture reporting is treating the whole value chain as one undifferentiated 'farmer satisfaction' measure.
section_rules.discussion: Explicitly discuss whether a production-side gain (e.g. yield improvement) is translating into an income-side gain -- this distinction is the most common and most important analytical thread in agriculture M&E and must never be assumed without evidence.
section_rules.methodology: State the agricultural season/cycle the survey covers, since agriculture findings are highly season-dependent and non-comparable across different points in a growing cycle without this context.
section_rules.limitations: Note whether yield/income figures are farmer-reported estimates or independently verified (e.g. against sales records) -- self-reported yield estimates carry meaningfully more uncertainty than verified figures.
section_rules.recommendations: Separate input-access recommendations (fertilizer, seed) from market-linkage recommendations (pricing, aggregation, cooperatives) -- these require different partners and interventions.
section_rules.policy_implications: Escalate to policy level only for findings with cross-district consistency (e.g. a market pricing structure issue affecting multiple districts), not single-cooperative or single-market anomalies.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Reflect on whether open-ended feedback revealed a value-chain gap (e.g. yield success not converting to income) that a yield-only survey design would have missed entirely.
section_rules.conclusion: Avoid declaring an agricultural intervention an unqualified success based on yield alone -- always note whether the income/market dimension was also assessed and what it showed.
section_rules.annexes: Include the specific crop(s) and season referenced, since agricultural findings are not comparable across different crops or seasons without this context.
section_rules.evidence_style: Ground every yield or income claim in the specific topic mention count and, where available, a specific respondent quote attributing the change to a specific input or intervention.

common_risks: self-reported yield estimates without independent verification
seasonal confounding when comparing rounds from different points in the growing cycle
assuming yield improvement automatically implies income improvement
typical_recommendations: market aggregation or off-take agreements to improve farm-gate pricing
farm-gate price tracking as a standing indicator alongside yield
typical_policy_implications: market infrastructure and cooperative development policy
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Agriculture donors (FAO/IFAD-aligned) increasingly expect income-level evidence, not yield-level evidence alone, given a widely recognized gap between production gains and farmer income gains across the sector.

forbidden_behaviors: Never claim a yield finding demonstrates an income improvement without a specific income/market-price data point supporting that claim.
Never cite a specific FAO or IFAD guidance document without independent verification that it exists and applies.
Never compare yield findings across different crops or seasons as if directly equivalent.
